title: GenAI System Design
description: Design problems for enterprise assistants, document intelligence, AI search, coding assistants, memory, and evaluation platforms.

questions.title: Design ChatGPT for enterprise documents.
questions.scenario: A company wants a conversational assistant over millions of internal documents.
questions.expectedAnswer: Cover document ingestion, permissions, parsing, chunking, retrieval, reranking, answer generation, citations, conversation state, feedback, observability, model routing, and governance. Discuss non-functional requirements such as latency, scale, security, cost, availability, and freshness. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A company wants a conversational assistant over millions of internal documents. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by turning the concept into architecture: define inputs, data flow, ownership boundaries, controls, evaluation signals, observability, and rollout steps for genai system design.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "Design ChatGPT for enterprise documents.", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real genai system design setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Skipping the scenario and giving a generic textbook explanation.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for GenAI System Design?
What would change at enterprise scale?
What failure modes would you monitor?
questions.realWorldExample: In an enterprise genai system design setting, the team should connect the answer to user workflow, governance, observability, and measurable business impact.
questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: Design a document intelligence platform.
questions.scenario: An insurance company processes claims documents, forms, and invoices.
questions.expectedAnswer: Include upload, OCR, layout-aware parsing, table extraction, classification, entity extraction, human review, storage, search, RAG, confidence scoring, and audit logs. Discuss accuracy, throughput, latency, compliance, and integration with downstream workflows. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: An insurance company processes claims documents, forms, and invoices. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by turning the concept into architecture: define inputs, data flow, ownership boundaries, controls, evaluation signals, observability, and rollout steps for genai system design.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "Design a document intelligence platform.", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real genai system design setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

questions.title: Design AI search over 500 million documents.
questions.scenario: A global enterprise wants semantic and keyword search across all knowledge repositories.
questions.expectedAnswer: Discuss ingestion pipelines, sharding, metadata, lexical and vector indexes, hybrid retrieval, reranking, caching, access control, query understanding, evaluation, freshness, and cost. Scale choices should cover index partitioning, recall-latency tradeoffs, and monitoring. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A global enterprise wants semantic and keyword search across all knowledge repositories. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.

answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "Design AI search over 500 million documents.", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real genai system design setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

questions.title: Design an enterprise memory system for AI assistants.
questions.scenario: A productivity assistant should remember preferences and project context safely.
questions.expectedAnswer: Cover memory types, write policies, retrieval policies, consent, privacy, retention, correction, tenant isolation, user controls, summarization, and evaluation. Strong answers avoid storing everything and distinguish user memory from task state. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: A productivity assistant should remember preferences and project context safely. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.

questions.title: Design an evaluation platform for GenAI apps.
questions.scenario: Many teams need to compare prompts and models before production rollout.
questions.expectedAnswer: Include dataset management, prompt/model versioning, test runs, LLM-as-judge, human review, metrics, trace replay, regression gates, dashboards, and deployment integration. Discuss calibration, cost, reproducibility, and business-specific rubrics. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

questions.title: Design around secure RAG platform.
questions.scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through secure RAG platform for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer should define secure RAG platform, explain why it matters for genai system design, and connect it to a practical implementation decision. The candidate should describe the core mechanism, identify where it appears in a production system, discuss tradeoffs such as quality, cost, latency, safety, governance, and maintainability, and name how they would evaluate whether the approach is working. For senior or architect-level interviews, the answer should also include failure modes, ownership boundaries, and rollout considerations. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

questions.interviewerNotes: A strong candidate should give a structured answer, make assumptions explicit, discuss tradeoffs, and explain how they would test the decision rather than relying on buzzwords.

questions.title: Design around AI workflow automation platform.
questions.scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through AI workflow automation platform for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter.
questions.expectedAnswer: A strong answer should define AI workflow automation platform, explain why it matters for genai system design, and connect it to a practical implementation decision. The candidate should describe the core mechanism, identify where it appears in a production system, discuss tradeoffs such as quality, cost, latency, safety, governance, and maintainability, and name how they would evaluate whether the approach is working. For senior or architect-level interviews, the answer should also include failure modes, ownership boundaries, and rollout considerations. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: An interviewer asks you to reason through AI workflow automation platform for an enterprise AI use case where correctness, cost, security, and adoption all matter. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by turning the concept into architecture: define inputs, data flow, ownership boundaries, controls, evaluation signals, observability, and rollout steps for genai system design.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "Design around AI workflow automation platform.", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real genai system design setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

commonMistakes: Answering with definitions only and not connecting to a practical system.
Skipping the scenario and giving a generic textbook explanation.
Ignoring tradeoffs such as cost, latency, quality, safety, and maintainability.
Failing to mention how the approach would be evaluated in production.
followUpQuestions: How would you validate this for GenAI System Design?
What would change at enterprise scale?
